skill.name: lark-shared
skill.description: Guides Lark CLI setup, authentication, identity selection, permission troubleshooting, scope handling, and safe command use for Lark resources.
skill.summary: Use Lark CLI safely for setup, login, permissions, scopes, and user or bot identity selection.
skill.author: larksuite

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backtick command patterns, a weak cryptography signal, and high entropy in SKILL.md. Review found Markdown command examples and Chinese text, not Ruby execution, cryptographic code, obfuscated content, malware, or prompt injection. The remaining risk is legitimate but sensitive external Lark CLI use for configuration and authentication flows.

medium_findings.title: External Lark CLI Authentication Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run Lark CLI configuration and auth login commands. These commands are legitimate for the skill purpose, but they can start authorization flows and affect account or app access if used without user intent confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and tied to Lark setup and OAuth-style authorization. The file also includes safeguards, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: The backtick detections occur in Markdown inline code, tables, and fenced examples for lark-cli usage. I did not find Ruby code, shell interpolation, user-controlled command construction, or hidden execution logic in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown guidance and examples, not executable Ruby or script files. The single file contains no code wrapper that would execute these snippets automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Signal Appears False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding points to the description line, which mentions auth login and CLI setup. I found no hash function, cipher, random generation, or cryptographic implementation at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 4 is frontmatter text, not code. There is no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithm use in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: High Entropy Signal Appears Caused by Multilingual Markdown
low_findings.description: The entropy finding points to the start of SKILL.md. The file is readable Markdown with Chinese and English text, not binary, encrypted, or obfuscated content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed clear YAML frontmatter and human-readable instructions. No encoded payload, compressed blob, or hidden script was present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-Initiated Authorization Flow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends running auth login in the background and sending an authorization link to the user. This pattern should require clear user intent, least-privilege scope selection, and secret redaction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The authorization flow is directly described and includes a concrete command. The same file contains safeguards, which reduces but does not remove the operational risk.

content.value_statement: Lark CLI setup can fail when identity, scopes, or permissions are unclear. This skill gives structured guidance for setup, login, scope repair, and safer command handling.
actual_capabilities: Guides first-time Lark CLI configuration with app setup commands.
Explains when to use user identity or bot identity with --as.
Helps interpret permission denied errors and missing scope messages.
Recommends scoped auth login commands for least-privilege authorization.
Lists safety rules for secrets, write actions, and dry-run previews.
limitations: Does not provide Lark credentials, app secrets, or access tokens.
Requires the lark-cli tool to be installed and configured locally.
Cannot grant permissions without user or administrator action in Lark.
Does not replace Lark platform documentation for product-specific API behavior.

use_cases.title: First-time CLI setup
use_cases.description: Set up a Lark app configuration and understand the authorization link flow before using CLI commands.

use_cases.title: Permission error repair
use_cases.description: Identify missing scopes from CLI errors and choose the correct bot or user permission fix.

use_cases.title: Identity-aware automation
use_cases.description: Select user or bot identity before reading calendars, documents, messages, or other Lark resources.

prompt_templates.title: Start Lark CLI setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me initialize Lark CLI configuration. Explain what command will run and what I need to do with the authorization link.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an identity
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to access a Lark resource with lark-cli. Help me decide whether to use --as user or --as bot before running commands.

prompt_templates.title: Fix missing permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Lark CLI permission error and tell me which scope or console action is needed. Keep the recommendation least-privilege.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a safe CLI operation
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Lark CLI workflow that may write or delete data. Include identity choice, required scopes, confirmation points, and dry-run checks.

output_examples.input: I am new to lark-cli and need setup help.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the configuration command, notes that it may wait for authorization, and asks the user to complete the link-based setup.

output_examples.input: A command failed because a calendar scope is missing.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies whether the current identity is user or bot, then recommends either console scope setup or scoped user login.

output_examples.input: I want to delete Lark data from a script.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for explicit confirmation, recommends a dry-run preview, and checks that the selected identity has only required permissions.

best_practices: Confirm the intended Lark identity before running commands that access resources.
Use the narrowest scope that fixes the current permission error.
Keep app secrets and access tokens out of terminal output and shared logs.
anti_patterns: Do not run auth login for bot identity when console scope setup is required.
Do not request broad domain access when one missing scope is enough.
Do not perform write or delete operations without explicit user confirmation.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps configure Lark CLI, select user or bot identity, handle scopes, and troubleshoot permission errors.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: It provides command guidance. Any sensitive setup, auth, write, or delete action should require clear user approval.

faq.question: When should I use user identity?
faq.answer: Use user identity when accessing personal resources, such as a user calendar, cloud files, or mailbox data.

faq.question: When should I use bot identity?
faq.answer: Use bot identity for app-level actions where the app has the needed scopes in the Lark developer console.

faq.question: How should permission denied errors be handled?
faq.answer: Check the missing scope, console link, and current identity. Then apply the smallest permission change that solves the task.

faq.question: Does this skill expose secrets?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly warns against printing app secrets or access tokens in terminal output.
